target.name: Zeta Surgical
target.founder: Jose Amich, Raahil Sha, William Gormley

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $100M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Biotech & Health. Range: $600M - $3.3B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Biotech & Health P/S median is 5.8x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Biotech & Health
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Biotech & Health
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Biotech & Health

data.narrative:
Zeta Surgical is a Series A-stage biotech company valued at $100M post-money following $23M in total capital raised. The valuation analysis draws on a universe of 8 private peers and 3 public comparables in the Biotech & Health sector, including Series B-stage companies such as Eikon Therapeutics, Neko Health, New Limit, Asimov, and Profluent.

Two triangulation methods proved applicable. Stage-progression analysis, anchored to the $100M last post-money valuation with a typical 2.0x Series A-to-next-stage step-up multiplier, implies a forward valuation of $200M. Peer-median analysis across the five same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables yields a median valuation of $810M, with a range spanning $600M to $3.3B. Public-comp anchoring could not be applied due to the absence of revenue data for the target company, which is standard for private-stage disclosure. The 3-ticker public Biotech & Health universe carries a median P/S multiple of 5.8x.

The two applicable methods triangulate to a range of $170M to $931M, with a median implied valuation of $505M. The wide spread reflects material uncertainty around early-stage biotech risk and heterogeneity within the peer set, particularly given the stage heterogeneity of comparables (Series B vs. Series A). Key sensitivities include clinical progress, capital efficiency, and whether peer valuations reflect durable technology differentiation or sector-wide momentum.
